content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 .J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Douglas L. White 
Churchwell White LLP 
1414 K Street, 3n1 Floor 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-029 
Dear Mr. White: 
March 9, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Dixon Mayor Thom 
Bogue regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please 
note that this advice does not analyze any other area of law, including Section 1090, the Public 
Contract Code, or common law conflicts of interest. Please also note that we are not a finder of fact 
when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes 
your facts are complete and accurate. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit the Mayor from taking part in governmental decisions regarding a 
potential conditional use permit for a proposed automotive repair and restoration business, given 
that the Mayor owns and operates First Choice Automotive, an automotive repair and vehicle 
emissions business in close proximity to the site of the proposed business? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The Act prohibits the Mayor from taking part in decisions relating to the potential 
conditional use permit for the proposed business because those decisions would have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on the Mayor's financial interest in First Choice Automotive. 
FACTS 
You are the Dixon City Attorney and the Mayor's authorized representative. The City 
Council will soon consider whether to approve a conditional use permit authorizing a proposed 
automotive repair and restoration business to operate at 160 South First Street within the City. The 
1 The Political Reform Act is con1ained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-17-029 
Page No. 2 
Mayor was elected to office on November 8, 2016, and did not hold elected office during the City's 
consideration of the conditional use permit to date. 
The Mayor owns and operates First Choic;e Automotive, an automotive repair and vehicle 
emissions business, located at 1 JO South Porter Road within the City. First Choice Automotive 
formerly rented 160 South First Street from Connie Lewis, the applicant for the conditional use 
permit. Upon the termination of First Choice Automotive· s tenancy with Ms. Lewis in April 2 014, 
First Choice Automotive moved to its new location on South Porter Road where it remains. 
You state that the proposed automotive repair and restoration business would be within 
approximately 1,500 to 2,000 feet of First Choice Automotive if the City approves the conditional 
use permit. According to Google Maps, although there are numerous businesses that provide 
automotive repair services throughout the City, currently none of those businesses are within the 
immediate vicinity of First Choice Automotive. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in the making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial 
interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests 
identified in that section. The Mayor has an interest in First Choice Automotive because he owns 
and operates that business. (See Section 87103(a) and (d).) 
Foreseeability & Materiality 
The effect of a decision before the official's agency is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable if the official's interest is explicitly involved in the decision. (Regulation l 870l(a).) An 
official's interest is explicitly involved in a decision if it is a named party in, or the subject of, the 
decision. (Ibid.) For an interest not explicitly involved in a decision, the decision's effect is 
reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. (Regulation 1870l(b).) The Mayor's interest in First Choice Automotive is not 
explicitly involved in the decisions at issue because First Choice Automotive is not a named party 
in, or the subject of, those decisions. Therefore, the effect of the decisions is reasonably foreseeable 
if it is a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
Regulation 18702.1 (b) provides that a decision's effect on an official's business interest is 
material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision's effect would contribute to a change in the value of the privately-held business entity. 
That regulation also provides various examples of the types of decisions that may impact the 
analysis, including the example of decisions that would increase or decrease the amount of 
competition in the field in which the business is engaged. (Regulation l 8702.l(b)(2).) 
First Choice Automotive provides automotive repair and vehicle emissions related services, 
and the proposed business would provide automotive repair and restoration services. Therefore, the 
businesses are in competition to the extent that they both provide automotive repair services. 


File No. A-17-029 
Page No. 3 
Although there are numerous businesses that provide automotive repair services throughout 
the City, currently none of those businesses are within the immediate vicinity of First Choice 
Automotive. You state that the proposed automotive repair and restoration business would be 
within approximately 1,500 to 2,000 feet of First Choice Automotive if the City approves the 
conditional use permit. Based on these facts, the decisions regarding the conditional use pennil for 
the proposed automotive repair and restoration business would contribute to a change in the value 
of First Choice Automotive by increasing the amount of competition in its field. 
Accordingly, we conclude that the Act prohibits the Mayor from taking part in decisions 
regarding the potential conditional use permit for the proposed business because those decisions 
would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Mayor's financial interest in 
First Choice Automotive. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit the Mayor from taking part in governmental decisions regarding a 
potential conditional use permit for a proposed automotive repair and restoration business, given 
that the Mayor owns and operates First Choice Automotive, an automotive repair and vehicle 
emissions business in close proximity to the site of the proposed business?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. The Act prohibits the Mayor from taking part in decisions relating to the potential 
conditional use permit for the proposed business because those decisions would have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on the Mayor's financial interest in First Choice Automotive.

Although there are numerous businesses that provide automotive repair services throughout

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit the Mayor from taking part in governmental decisions regarding a 
potential conditional use permit for a proposed automotive repair and restoration business, given 
that the Mayor owns and operates First Choice Automotive, an automotive repair and vehicle 
emissions business in close proximity to the site of the proposed business?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The Act prohibits the Mayor from taking part in decisions relating to the potential 
conditional use permit for the proposed business because those decisions would have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on the Mayor's financial interest in First Choice Automotive.
